items.question_text: Under 3-10-1, landing information must include the runway, wind, and what other key element?
items.explanation: Altimeter setting is a mandatory element of landing information.

choices.text: altimeter setting

items.question_text: Under 3-10-2, nonapproach control towers must forward what to arriving aircraft?
items.explanation: Nonapproach control towers forward approach information such as the type of approach to expect, which helps pilots plan their arrival.

choices.text: Forward approach information such as the type of approach to expect.
Forward a full weather briefing including all NOTAMs.
Forward the complete flight plan amendment with the expected route.
Forward only the ATIS code — nothing else is needed.

items.question_text: Complete: Do not permit an aircraft to cross the landing threshold until the preceding aircraft has _____ the runway.
items.explanation: Same-runway arrival separation requires the preceding aircraft to have 'cleared' the runway before the next crosses the threshold.

choices.text: cleared

items.question_text: Under 3-10-4, what type of separation must be ensured for intersecting runway arrivals?
items.explanation: Arriving aircraft on intersecting runways must be separated so they do not arrive at the intersection at the same time.

choices.text: Radar separation of at least 5 miles.
The aircraft must not arrive at the runway intersection at the same time.
Altitude separation of at least 1,000 feet until on final.
Only visual separation between the two aircraft.

items.question_text: Complete: The landing clearance need not be withheld if the pilot cannot see the runway but can see which lights?
items.explanation: The approach lights (ALS) or other identifiable portions of the airport are sufficient for the landing clearance to be issued, even if the runway itself is not yet visible.

choices.text: approach lights

items.question_text: Under 3-10-6, when can a landing clearance be issued while the preceding aircraft is still on the runway?
items.explanation: Anticipating separation allows issuing a landing clearance when the preceding aircraft will have cleared the runway before the next aircraft crosses the landing threshold. This is the arrival analogue of 3-9-5 for departures.

choices.text: Only when the preceding aircraft has come to a complete stop.
When the preceding aircraft will be clear of the runway before the arrival crosses the threshold.
Never — a landing clearance cannot be issued until the runway is verified clear.
Only when the visibility is at least 3 miles and both aircraft can see each other.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-7, a landing clearance may be issued without visually observing the runway when what conditions are met?
items.explanation: When the runway is not visible from the tower, a landing clearance may be issued provided separation requirements are met and the aircraft reports the runway in sight or the controller can determine the runway is clear through other means.

choices.text: Only when the pilot has a functioning autoland system and the tower has a working PAR.
When separation requirements are met and either the pilot reports the runway in sight or the controller can otherwise determine it is clear.
Only between sunrise and sunset when visibility is unrestricted.
Never — visual observation of the runway is always required before issuing a landing clearance.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-8, when must a landing clearance be withheld?
items.explanation: A landing clearance must be withheld when the landing runway is occupied by another aircraft or vehicle, or when other safety conditions prevent safe landing.

choices.text: When the aircraft is more than 10 miles from the airport.
When the pilot has not yet reported the ATIS code.
When the landing runway is occupied by another aircraft or vehicle.
Only when the weather is below basic VFR minima.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-9, after landing, how should a controller instruct an aircraft to exit the runway?
items.explanation: The controller must give specific taxi instructions for exiting the runway, including which taxiway to use. The aircraft should not exit at an arbitrary point without instruction.

choices.text: Wait for the pilot to select any available taxiway and report when clear.
Issue specific instructions for exiting the runway, including which taxiway to use.
Use only the light gun to signal the aircraft off the runway.
Allow the aircraft to exit at the first available taxiway without further instruction.

items.question_text: Complete: An altitude restricted low approach is a low approach with a specified altitude restriction lower than the published _____ altitude.
items.explanation: The restriction is lower than the published minimum altitude, making this a controlled deviation from the standard approach minima.

choices.text: minimum

items.question_text: Under 3-10-11, closed traffic operations require the controller to establish what before clearing subsequent aircraft?
items.explanation: The sequence must be established first. Closed traffic operations involve repeated patterns and need sequencing just like any other traffic.

choices.text: sequence

items.question_text: Under 3-10-12, the overhead maneuver begins with the aircraft approaching from which direction relative to the runway?
items.explanation: The overhead maneuver involves the aircraft approaching from upwind, then breaking to downwind for landing. It's a military pattern maneuver.

choices.text: Directly from the side, perpendicular to the runway.
From upwind, breaking to downwind for landing.
From below the pattern, climbing to join the downwind.
From the opposite direction, reversing course over the runway.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-13, what is a Simulated Flameout (SFO) approach?
items.explanation: An SFO approach simulates an engine failure by descending steeply from high altitude to the runway. It is a military training maneuver, not an actual emergency.

choices.text: An actual engine-out emergency requiring full crash-response preparation.
A practice maneuver simulating an engine failure with a steep descent to landing.
A standard straight-in ILS approach flown by military pilots.
A low-level tactical approach pattern for combat operations.
